items.q: Is a radioactive sample completely gone after two half-lives?
items.correct: No — each half-life halves what remains, so one quarter is still undecayed after two half-lives.
items.misconception: Yes — two halves make the whole sample decay.
items.random_wrong: Yes — all decay stops after one half-life.
items.why: Students add halves linearly instead of halving the remainder repeatedly — decay is exponential.

items.q: Why did some alpha particles bounce back in Rutherford's experiment?
items.correct: They approached a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus almost head-on and were repelled.
items.misconception: They collided with electrons spread throughout the atom.
items.random_wrong: They reflected off the shiny gold surface.
items.why: Students credit electrons, but electrons are far too light; only a concentrated positive nucleus could turn an alpha around.

items.q: Is mass exactly conserved in nuclear reactions?
items.correct: No — a small mass difference (the mass defect) is converted to energy according to E = mc².
items.misconception: Yes — total mass never changes in any reaction.
items.random_wrong: No — about half the mass disappears in every reaction.
items.why: Conservation of mass from chemistry is overgeneralised; nuclear binding energy comes from a real, measurable mass change.

items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space with a small, dense, positively charged nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Electrons sit inside a uniform sphere of positive charge.
items.why: Correct answer: Most of the atom is empty space with a small, dense, positively charged nucleus.. “Alpha particles are negatively charged.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: After two half-lives, the fraction of the original nuclei still undecayed is:
items.why: It's 1/4. “1/2” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's becquerels (Bq). “grays (Gy)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Energy is released in fission because:
items.correct: The fragments have a higher binding energy per nucleon than the original heavy nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Mass is created during the split.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The fragments have a higher binding energy per nucleon than the original heavy nucleus.. “Neutrons have no mass.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A source has a half-life of 8 days. What percentage of the nuclei remain undecayed after 16 days?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
